title: Difficult Terrain
excerpt: Difficult terrain is ground, material, or space that costs extra movement to cross because it resists clear, efficient travel.
markdown:
# Difficult Terrain

Difficult terrain is ground, material, or space that slows movement because a creature cannot cross it as efficiently as open, stable ground. It changes how much distance a creature can cover with its available movement, even though it does not change that creature's base [[speed]].

## Rules

- moving through difficult terrain costs extra movement compared with crossing clear ground
- the extra cost reflects unstable footing, entangling growth, rubble, deep snow, mud, shallow water, magical hindrance, or another feature that resists normal travel
- a creature can still move through difficult terrain if it has enough movement remaining to pay the added cost
- difficult terrain changes how far movement can carry a creature; it does not create a separate condition on its own

## Sources Of Difficult Terrain

Difficult terrain can come from natural ground, constructed obstacles, hazards, conjured matter, or magical effects that make movement slower, less stable, or harder to route cleanly.

## Movement Pressure

Difficult terrain matters most when position, distance, and remaining movement are already constrained. It can shorten how far a creature advances, reduce how much repositioning fits inside a turn, and make retreat or pursuit more expensive.

## Related Movement Rules

Extra movement from [[dash_action|Dash [Action]]] can help cross difficult terrain, but the terrain still consumes that movement faster than normal. Broader movement rules such as [[travel_pace|Travel Pace]], [[climbing]], and [[swimming]] can stack their own pressures on top of difficult terrain when the world state supports both.
